speaker: Unknown
en: My most controversial opinion is that I think that AI is as big a deal as the internet or mobile, and only as big a deal as the internet or mobile.
zh: 我最具争议的观点是，我认为 AI 像互联网或移动技术一样重要，但也只像互联网或移动技术那样重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: But you're just on the coming job apocalypse.
zh: 但你只是赶上了即将到来的职场末日。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every time we have a new technology, it automates away a bunch of jobs.
zh: 每次我们拥有一项新技术，它都会自动化掉一大批工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then that automation unlocks a bunch of new jobs.
zh: 然后那种自动化会解锁一大批新工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you don't know the new job because it doesn't exist yet.
zh: 而且你不知道那份新工作，因为它还不存在。

speaker: Unknown
en: We've had that process over and over again.
zh: 我们已经一遍又一遍地经历过这个过程。

speaker: Unknown
en: Even just looking at the most advanced AI companies, throughout big OpenAI, everyone's increasing headcount.
zh: 即使只是看看最先进的 AI 公司，在整个大型 OpenAI，每个人都在增加编制。

speaker: Unknown
en: You talk to these doomers on Twitter and they would act like every big company is going to buy ChatGPT tomorrow, and then in two weeks' time, they'll fire all their stuff.
zh: 你在 Twitter 上和那些末日论者交谈，他们会表现得好像每家大公司明天都要购买 ChatGPT，然后在两周时间内，他们会解雇所有的员工。

speaker: Unknown
en: These people are more on.
zh: 这些人更是在线。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can't predict which things are going to be exposed.
zh: 你无法预测哪些东西将会被暴露出来。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can't look at a senior partner at a law firm and say, "Well, 17% of their work could be automated."
zh: 你不能看着一家律师事务所的高级合伙人说，「嗯，他们工作中 17% 的部分可以被自动化。」

speaker: Unknown
en: This is horseshit.
zh: 这是胡扯。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm curious if you're following the anti-AI sentiment.
zh: 我很好奇你是否关注反 AI 情绪。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a big fuzzy mess.
zh: 这是一团巨大的模糊混乱。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes, this will change a bunch of stuff and we'll need to worry about it, but that's kind of a constant.
zh: 是的，这会改变很多事情，我们需要对此表示担忧，但这有点像是一种常态。

speaker: Unknown
en: We've always had that.
zh: 我们一直都有这种事。

speaker: Unknown
en: What would be a couple things you recommend people do to be more successful in this future?
zh: 关于你建议人们做些什么以便在这个未来取得更多成功，会有哪几件事？

speaker: Unknown
en: Don't stick your head in the sand and say, "I hate all of this stuff."
zh: 不要把头埋在沙子里说，「我讨厌所有的这些东西。」

speaker: Unknown
en: That gives you a great feeling of all superiority and you can go on BlueSky and chat at everybody about how Evil AI is like, great, happy for you.
zh: 那会给你一种极好的优越感，你可以去 BlueSky 和大家聊天，说说邪恶的 AI 是怎样的，就像，很棒，为你高兴。

speaker: Unknown
en: But that's not going to help.
zh: 但那不会有帮助。

speaker: Unknown
en: What helps is you diving into this and coming out, understanding what you can do with it.
zh: 有帮助的是你深入其中并走出来，理解你能用它做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today, my guest is Benedict Evans.
zh: 今天，我的嘉宾是 Benedict Evans。

speaker: Unknown
en: Benedict was a longtime partner at A16Z as their in house analyst and resident thinker.
zh: Benedict 曾是 A16Z 的长期合伙人，担任他们的内部分析师和常驻思想家。

speaker: Unknown
en: Before that, he was a longtime equity researcher.
zh: 在那之前，他是一名长期的股票研究员。

speaker: Unknown
en: And for the past six years, he's been an independent analyst tracking the most important tech trends and sharing what he's learning.
zh: 在过去六年里，他是一名独立分析师，追踪最重要的技术趋势并分享他所学到的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most recently, as you'd expect, he's spending all his time on how AI is changing our lives.
zh: 最近，正如你所料，他把所有时间都花在 AI 如何改变我们的生活上。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in his words, "AI is eating the world."
zh: 用他的话来说，「AI 正在吞噬世界。」

speaker: Unknown
en: In this conversation, we go deep on what we're still not pricing in on the impact that AI is going to have on our lives and our work, the rise of anti-AI sentiment, the impact on jobs, where in the value chain most of the value will accrue, and tons more.
zh: 在这次对话中，我们深入探讨了我们尚未计入定价的影响，即 AI 将对我们的生活和我们的工作产生的影响，反 AI 情绪的兴起，对工作的影响，价值链中大部分价值将累积在何处，以及更多内容。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you are worried about AI, or just confused about where things are heading, this conversation will teach you a lot, and also make you feel better.
zh: 如果你担心 AI，或者只是对事情的发展方向感到困惑，这次对话将会教会你很多，也会让你感觉好一些。

speaker: Unknown
en: Before we get into it, don't forget to check out Lenny'sproductpass.com for a year free of some of the most amazing, hottest, most well-crafted AI products in the world, available exclusively to Lenny's newsletter subscribers.
zh: 在我们开始之前，别忘了去查看 Lenny'sproductpass.com，免费一年获取一些世界上最神奇、最热门、最精心制作的 AI 产品，专供 Lenny 的新闻订阅者使用。

speaker: Unknown
en: With that, I bring you Benedict Evans.
zh: 话虽如此，我为您带来 Benedict Evans。

speaker: Unknown
en: Benedict, thank you so much for being here.
zh: Benedict，非常感谢你的到来。

speaker: Unknown
en: Welcome to the podcast.
zh: 欢迎来到播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Thank you for inviting me.
zh: 感谢你的邀请。

speaker: Unknown
en: You just put out this deck called AI is Eating the World.
zh: 你刚刚发布了这个名为《AI 正在吞噬世界》的幻灯片。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to ask you the flip side of this, of we all know it's a big deal.
zh: 我想问问你这个的反面，我们都知道这是一件大事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Knowing that, what do you think people are still not fully pricing in when they think about the change that they're going to experience to their lives and their work?
zh: 知道了这一点，你认为人们在思考他们将要在生活和工作中经历的变化时，仍然没有完全计入定价的是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: An interesting way of thinking about it, I did a podcast last year with someone where I said my most controversial opinion is that I think that AI is as big a deal as the internet or mobile.
zh: 一个有趣的思考方式，去年我和某人做了一次播客，我说我最具争议的观点是，我认为 AI 像互联网或移动技术一样重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: And only as big a deal as the internet or mobile, because clearly there's a bunch of people in tech who think, no, this is more like the industrial revolution or something.
zh: 而且只像互联网或移动技术那样重要，因为很明显科技界有一群人认为，不，这更像是工业革命之类的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there are a whole bunch of people underneath saying, "Well, he thinks this is just as big as...
zh: 下面有一大堆人说，「嗯，他认为这就像……

speaker: Unknown
en: Does he not understand how big this is?"
zh: 他不明白这有多大吗？」

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm like, smartphones were quite a big deal.
zh: 而我就像说，智能手机是件大事。

speaker: Unknown
en: The internet was quite a big deal.
zh: 互联网是件大事。

speaker: Unknown
en: We wouldn't be doing this if it wasn't for the internet.
zh: 如果不是因为互联网，我们就不会做这个了。

speaker: Unknown
en: But then if you dig into that, if you're going to make the internet comparison, it's like we're a 1997.
zh: 但是如果你深挖这一点，如果你要做互联网的比较，这就像我们在 1997 年。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very exciting.
zh: 这非常令人兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most stuff doesn't work yet.
zh: 大多数东西还不起作用。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most of the stuff that people are going to do hasn't been built yet, and it's not really clear how any of it's going to work when it does work.
zh: 人们将要做的大部分事情还没有建立起来，而且当它起作用时，它的运作方式还不清楚。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the people who've already got it, who have already taken whichever pill it is, I forget which, sort of imagine that everybody in the world is already there.
zh: 那些已经得到它、已经服下某种药丸的人——我忘了是哪一种——有点想象世界上的每个人都已经到了那里。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the truth is you've got this kind of very wide distribution.
zh: 事实是你得到了这种非常广泛的分布。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's people in tech who bought their cluster of Mac minis, and don't use Google anymore.
zh: 所以在科技界有一些人买了他们的 Mac mini 集群，并且不再使用 Google 了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then you look out side tech and setting aside the idiots who think that this isn't real.
zh: 然后你看科技界以外，把那些认为这不是真的白痴放在一边。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most people who are using this are using this every week or shoot, maybe.
zh: 大多数使用这个的人每周都在使用这个，或者天哪，也许吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you've got that kind of spread of adoption and that spread of maturity of how well this works.
zh: 所以你就有那种采用程度的分布，以及它运作良好的成熟度的分布。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then within that, you can make specific points about, well, how are the models going to work?
zh: 然后在那其中，你可以提出具体的观点，比如，模型将如何运作？

speaker: Unknown
en: And do the model labs have pricing power?
zh: 模型实验室有定价权吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And where's the value going to be?
zh: 价值将去向何方？

speaker: Unknown
en: And has OpenAI won the whole thing?
zh: OpenAI 已经赢下了整个局吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Or is Anthropic got it this week?
zh: 还是 Anthropic 本周赢了？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so then you can kind of get into calling those races where, again, it's like being in 1997 and saying, "Well, is it going to be Excite or Yahoo?"
zh: 所以然后你可以开始谈论那些竞赛，同样，这就像在 1997 年说，“好吧，会是 Excite 还是 Yahoo？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And the answer was no, generally.
zh: 答案是不，通常来说。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's a fractual point here.
zh: 所以这里有一个分形点。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's the super high level that this is going to change absolutely everything.
zh: 在超级高层面上，这将改变绝对的一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think it's particularly productive to say, "Well, is it 20% bigger than the internet or 100%?"
zh: 我不认为说“好吧，它比互联网大 20% 还是 100%？”是特别富有成效的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Those aren't productive conversations.
zh: 这些都不是富有成效的对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it's one of those fundamental changes, but then you don't know how any of it's going to work.
zh: 但这是那些根本性变化之一，但你不知道它将如何运作。

speaker: Unknown
en: In fact, I just published this.
zh: 实际上，我刚刚发布了这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I do a presentation every six months and I just published one yesterday, and one of the comments was, "Benedict, this is 80 slides of saying we don't know," which is slightly facetious, but also kind of true.
zh: 我每六个月做一次演示，我昨天刚发布了一个，有一条评论是，“Benedict，这是 80 张幻灯片在说我们不知道，”这有点说是俏皮话，但也有点真实。

zh: 本集由本季度的主要赞助商 WorkOS 为您带来。

zh: OpenAI、Anthropic、Cursor、Vercel、Replit、Sierra、Clay 和数百家其他成功公司有什么共同点？

speaker: Unknown
en: Visit workos.com to get started or just hit up their Slack where they have actual engineers waiting to answer your questions.

speaker: Unknown
en: Work OS allows you to build faster with delightful APIs, comprehensive docs, and a smooth developer experience.
zh: WorkOS 允许你通过令人愉快的 API、全面的文档和流畅的开发者体验更快地构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: Go to workos.com to make your app enterprise ready today.
zh: 前往 workos.com 让你的应用今天就为企业做好准备。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, if we're in this 1997 timeline for AI, I know so much of your messages we don't know where it's going exactly at.
zh: 所以，如果我们处于 AI 的这个 1997 年时间线，我知道你的很多信息是我们不知道它到底会去哪里。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know, do you have a sense of just the timeline to, okay, now things are going to be radically changing?
zh: 我不知道，你对这个时间线有感觉吗，好吧，现在事情将发生根本性的变化？

speaker: Unknown
en: Where are we in that cycle?
zh: 我们处于那个周期的哪个阶段？

speaker: Unknown
en: You talk about all these different cycles we've been through.
zh: 你谈论我们要经历的所有这些不同的周期。

speaker: Unknown
en: How far are we from just like, "Wow, it's all different"?
zh: 我们离仅仅就像“哇，一切都不同了”还有多远？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, unquestionably, we are already in that moment in software, and then there's a conversation about, well, what does Agentic and AI software development, three separate things that merge together, mean for the future of the software industry?
zh: 嗯，毫无疑问，在软件方面我们已经处于那个时刻了，然后有一个关于，好吧，Agentic 和 AI 软件开发意味着什么，三个合并在一起的事情，对软件行业的未来意味着什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's one extreme which is no one really believes, which is, "hey, you'll just vibe create your in Stripe."
zh: 有一个极端没有人真正相信，即，“嘿，你只会 vibe 创建你的在 Stripe。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And no one actually believes that, although briefly you don't believe that, but clearly there's a whole bunch of questions about what this means for the software industry, and how much stuff you'll be able to do yourself, or how much more software there will be.
zh: 没有人真正相信那个，虽然你不相信那个，但显然有一大堆关于这对软件行业意味着什么的问题，以及你自己能做多少事情，或者会有更多的软件。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's one whole conversation.
zh: 那是一个完整的对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the other extreme is, if you're in a law firm, this is all very interesting, but how exactly do we use this, and how do we work out how not to be the next story that we've submitted something with hallucinations in it, and how many associates are we going to hire next year?
zh: 但另一个极端是，如果你在一家律师事务所，这一切都很有趣，但我们到底如何使用这个，我们要如何弄清楚如何避免成为下一个提交了包含幻觉东西的故事，明年我们要雇佣多少助理？

speaker: Unknown
en: What does this mean for us?
zh: 这对我们意味着什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the analogies I used in the presentation is, imagine you're an accountant seeing the first software spreadsheets in the late '70s, and this is mind-blowing.
zh: 我在演示中使用的类比之一是，想象你是一名会计师，在 70 年代末看到了第一个软件电子表格，这是令人兴奋的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You change the interest rate here, and all the other numbers change.
zh: 你在这里改变利率，所有其他数字都会改变。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it does a week of work for you in like 30 seconds.
zh: 它在 30 秒内为你做了一周的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we can talk about what that meant for the accounting industry, but clearly if you're an accountant, this is obviously mind-blowing.
zh: 我们可以谈论这对会计行业意味着什么，但显然如果你是会计师，这显然是令人兴奋的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But if you were a lawyer looking at that, or journalist looking at that, you'd think, "Well, that's very clever and my accountant should see this," but that's not what I do.
zh: 但如果你是看着那个的律师，或者看着那个的记者，你会想，“嗯，那很聪明，我的会计师应该看看这个，”但那不是我所做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I might use it for my time sheet next week if it didn't cost 10 or $15,000 to get the Apple 2 and the monitor and the printer to run it, which is what it costs if you adjust replacing.
zh: 我下周可能会用它来做我的时间表，如果获得 Apple 2 和显示器和打印机来运行它不需要花费 10,000 或 15,000 美元，这是如果你调整替换后的成本。

speaker: Unknown
en: But that's not what I do.
zh: 但那不是我所做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you need Word processor, which actually came very shortly afterwards.
zh: 你需要文字处理器，实际上它很快就出现了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that's sort of the moment that we're in of, there's some people like software developers are the accountant seeing VisiCalc.
zh: 所以这有点像我们处于的时刻，有些人像软件开发人员就像看到 VisiCalc 的会计师。

speaker: Unknown
en: "Oh, my God, this changes everything."
zh: “哦，天哪，这改变了一切。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Before VisiCalc and after VisiCalc, before Claude Code and after Claude Code.
zh: VisiCalc 之前和 VisiCalc 之后,Claude Code 之前和 Claude Code 之后。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of other people are picking it up using it to varying degrees, but slightly puzzled.
zh: 很多其他人正在接受它并在不同程度上使用它,但稍微有些困惑。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, there's a bunch of survey data that I put in the presentation that, even if you look at like 13 to 18 year olds or something, it's still like 15, 20% of people are daily active users, and another 20% are weekly active users.
zh: 所以,我在演示文稿中放入了一些调查数据,即使你看像 13 到 18 岁左右的人群,仍然有大约 15%、20% 的人是日活跃用户,还有 20% 是周活跃用户。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the other 60% of those people in that demographic how on you say they are not using this.
zh: 然后该人群中另外 60% 的人怎么说呢,他们没有使用这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, there's a sort of very widespread of who gets it, and a very wide...
zh: 所以,关于谁理解它有一种非常广泛的分布,以及一种非常宽的……

speaker: Unknown
en: Which I think also maps, this is kind of almost a separate point, maps to this sort of jagged frontier question of, where does this work?
zh: 我认为这也映射了,这几乎是一个单独的点,映射到了这种参差不齐的前沿问题,即它在哪里有效?

speaker: Unknown
en: Where does it not work?
zh: 它在哪里无效?

speaker: Unknown
en: Can you tell where it's going to work?
zh: 你能说出它将在哪里有效吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it intuitive to know where it would work?
zh: 知道它会在哪里有效是直觉吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: Can you tell after it worked?
zh: 在它有效之后你能看出来吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: Can you work out for yourself what you would do with this?
zh: 你能自己弄清楚你会用它做什么吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: And all of those intersect with your software developer.
zh: 所有这些都与你的软件开发者相交。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a lot of other people who are like, people are having a moment or they're not, or again, we're in that kind of 1997 moment of, "Okay, what is this?"
zh: 还有很多其他人,就像人们正在经历某种时刻,或者他们没有,或者再说一次,我们正处于那种 1997 年的时刻,"好吧,这是什么?"

speaker: Unknown
en: Along those lines, something you've been writing a bit about is, this unexpected investment in professional services/consulting services/forward deployed engineers, all the AI labs, at least the two big ones, OpenAI and Anthropic, are investing in buying massive consultancies and PE firms.
zh: 沿着这些思路,你最近写的一点东西是,这种对专业服务/咨询服务/前向部署工程师的意外投资,所有的 AI 实验室,至少是两大家,OpenAI 和 Anthropic,都在投资收购大型咨询公司和 PE 公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about just what's happening there, why that's happening.
zh: 谈谈那里发生了什么,为什么会发生这种情况。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, it's funny, I was kind of groping for a joke last night when I wrote my newsletter and couldn't quite get to land it.
zh: 嗯,很有趣,昨晚我在写我的通讯时有点摸索笑话,没能完全把它落地。

speaker: Unknown
en: But something like, we know the joke that a machine learning scientist is a statistician who lives in San Francisco.
zh: 但是就像,我们知道那个笑话,机器学习科学家就是一个住在旧金山的统计学家。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's something in there of like a forward deployed engineer is like an Accenture outsource software developer who lives in San Francisco, or works in San Francisco.
zh: 其中有点像,一个前向部署工程师就像是一个住在旧金山或在旧金山工作的埃森哲外包软件开发人员。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, joking apart, if you have any experience of professional services, companies do not have lots of people sitting around waiting to build a big new project, or do a big new piece of analysis, or build a big new piece of technology, or a new product, or work out how they're going to redesign their stores, or work out where the store should be, or try and work out why the churn is too high.
zh: 我是说,抛开笑话不谈,如果你有任何专业服务的经验,公司并没有很多人闲坐着等待构建一个大型新项目,或做一大块新的分析,或构建一大块新技术,或新产品,或弄清楚他们将如何重新设计他们的商店,或弄清楚商店应该在哪里,或试图弄清楚为什么流失率太高。

speaker: Unknown
en: All of those kinds of questions are reasons why you hire Bain, BCG, McKinsey on one side, or Accenture, Infosys, whoever on the other, or you hire a branding agency, or you hire an arch firm of architects or whatever.
zh: 所有这类问题是你为什么在一侧雇佣 Bain、BCG、McKinsey,或在另一侧雇佣 Accenture、Infosys 或其他公司,或者你雇佣品牌代理公司,或者你雇佣一家建筑师事务所或其他公司的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's always like, well, we could hire some architects, but why on earth would we want to have 15 architects on staff when we would just go and hire an architecture firm, and we just go and hire an ad agency?
zh: 总是就像,嗯,我们可以雇佣一些建筑师,但我们究竟为什么要拥有 15 名建筑师在编制内,而我们可以去雇佣一家建筑师事务所,我们只是去雇佣一家广告代理公司?

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we are supposed to completely reimagine all of the internal workflows of your company, and work out which of them could be automated really quickly with AI.
zh: 所以我们 supposed to 完全重新想象你公司的所有内部工作流,并弄清楚其中哪些可以非常快地用 AI 自动化。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a project.
zh: 这是一个项目。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a project that needs like five or 10 people to sit down and spend a month or two working it out.
zh: 这是一个需要 5 到 10 个人坐下来花一两个月时间弄清楚的项目。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then actually doing it is another project.
zh: 然后实际做它又是另一个项目。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, so you need to plug these three vertical systems into these two horizontal systems and build a bunch of new workflows and train people to do that.
zh: 好的,所以你需要把这三个垂直系统接入这两个水平系统,并构建一堆新的工作流并培训人员去做那件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, guess what?
zh: 嗯,你猜怎么着?

speaker: Unknown
en: Who's going to do that?
zh: 谁来做那件事?

speaker: Unknown
en: Because you don't have a bunch of people sitting around not doing anything.
zh: 因为你的确没有一堆人闲着什么都不做。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, on the one side, this is part of the model of some PE firms, which is that they provide support to their portfolio companies to do stuff.
zh: 所以,一方面,这是一些 PE 公司模式的一部分,即他们向其投资组合公司提供支持来做事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And on the other side, that's why you hire, depending on what you're trying to do, you hire Bain, or you hire Accenture, or you hire publicists to help you work that out.
zh: 另一方面,这就是为什么你雇佣,取决于你想做什么,你雇佣 Bain,或者你雇佣 Accenture,或者你雇佣宣传员来帮你弄清楚。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's really just funny about this trend is you would think consultants were going to be gone.
zh: 这个趋势真正有趣的是,你会认为顾问要消失了。

speaker: Unknown
en: No, we don't need all these people anymore.
zh: 不,我们不再需要所有这些人了。

speaker: Unknown
en: AI is going to do their work.
zh: AI 会做他们的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Instead, the most cutting edge AI labs are the ones most investing in these folks.
zh: 相反,最前沿的 AI 实验室是那些最投资这些人的实验室。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's pretty surprising.
zh: 我认为这非常令人惊讶。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, one of the strands in my presentation, so I split the presentation into three sections.
zh: 嗯,我演讲中的一个主题,所以我把演讲分成了三个部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a section on capital, which is basically, where is all this CapEx going and are the model labs going to have differentiation?
zh: 有一部分关于资本,基本上是,所有这些 CapEx 都去哪儿了,模型实验室会有差异化吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's a section on deployment, which is basically, what does it mean for the software industry?
zh: 然后有一部分关于部署,基本上是,这对软件行业意味着什么?

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the third section is, how does this change stuff?
zh: 第三部分是,这如何改变事物?

speaker: Unknown
en: And one of the sort of strands I tried to pull together in the section on change is, what's the hard part of the job?
zh: 我试图在变革部分汇集的一个线索是,工作中困难的部分是什么?

speaker: Unknown
en: Is the hard part of the job writing the code line by line?
zh: 工作中困难的部分是一行一行地写代码吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: Is the hard part of the job giving you the SKU, or making the PowerPoint?
zh: 工作中困难的部分是给你 SKU,或者制作 PowerPoint 吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: Or is the hard part of the job something else?
zh: 还是工作中困难的部分是别的什么?

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it the task or the job?
zh: 是任务还是工作?

speaker: Unknown
en: And pulling that apart, sometimes the task is the job.
zh: 把那拆分开,有时任务就是工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: The classic example is like an elevator attendant.
zh: 经典的例子就像电梯服务员。

speaker: Unknown
en: If I live in a building that has an attended elevator, we have a manual elevator, so there's no button.
zh: 如果我住在一个有服务员电梯的大楼里,我们有一个手动电梯,所以没有按钮。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a lever and the dormant drives you to your floor.
zh: 有一个杠杆, dormant drives 你到你的楼层。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a vertical speed cut.
zh: 它是一个垂直速度 cut。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like one of those cramps in San Francisco.
zh: 这就像旧金山的那种电梯机房。

speaker: Unknown
en: They drive you to the store, to your floor.
zh: 它们载着你到商店，到你所在的楼层。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then those all got automated after the '50s, and now you press a button, and pressing the button is a job.
zh: 然后那些在 50 年代后都自动化了，现在你按一个按钮，而按按钮就是一项工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there were some things where the button, the job was a task and the task got automated.
zh: 所以有些情况是，这个按钮，这项工作是一个任务，而该任务被自动化了。

speaker: Unknown
en: What happens much more, and this is why people talked about the Jevons paradox is this pricey elasticity, because the Jevons paradox is just price elasticity, applied price elasticity.
zh: 更常见的情况是，这也是人们谈论杰文斯悖论的原因，是这个价格弹性，因为杰文斯悖论就是价格弹性，应用的价格弹性。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you make it cheaper to do something, what happens?
zh: 如果你让做某事变得更便宜，会发生什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you do the same for less money?
zh: 你会用更少的钱做同样的事吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Or do you do more for the same amount of money?
zh: 还是用同样的钱做更多的事？

speaker: Unknown
en: Or do you do more for more money because you've got new ROI?
zh: 还是因为有了新的投资回报率而花更多的钱做更多的事？

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you look at something like the history of accounting, or indeed professional services, this is a joke I made on Twitter back when it was Twitter, was like, young people won't believe this, but before Excel, junior investment bankers worked really long hours.
zh: 如果你看看会计的历史，或者专业服务的话，这是我在还是 Twitter 的时候在 Twitter 上开的一个玩笑，就是，年轻人不会相信这个，但在 Excel 之前，初级投资银行家工作真的很长时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: And now thanks to Excel, Goldman's associates will work at lunchtime on Fridays.
zh: 现在多亏了 Excel，高盛的 associates 会在周五的午餐时间工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, well, why is that not what happened?
zh: 这就像，好吧，为什么不是那样发生的呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: You could make the same point as software development.
zh: 你可以对软件开发提出同样的观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Before IDEs, and libraries, and operating systems, developers had to write all the code.
zh: 在 IDE、库和操作系统之前，开发人员必须编写所有代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now if you write an iPhone app, 90% of the code is written for you by Apple.
zh: 现在如果你编写一个 iPhone 应用，90% 的代码是 Apple 为你编写的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Apple wrote the modem driver, and the graphics drivers, and the file system.
zh: Apple 编写了调制解调器驱动程序、图形驱动程序和文件系统。

speaker: Unknown
en: You don't need to write any of that.
zh: 你不需要编写任何这些东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, we've got intents as many engineers now.
zh: 所以，我们现在有同样多的工程师，因为 intents。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, no.
zh: 好吧，不。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so then you kind of have to look at an industry and work out, well, which is it and what is the hard part?
zh: 所以你必须审视一个行业并弄清楚，是哪一种以及困难的部分是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the analogies that occurred to me here is to look at the history of e-commerce, which is that what Amazon does is it gets you the SKU if you know what the SKU is.
zh: 这里的一个类比是看电子商务的历史，也就是亚马逊所做的是如果你知道 SKU 是什么，它就为你获取该 SKU。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you know what SKU you want, you want that microphone stand.
zh: 如果你知道你想要什么 SKU，你想要那个麦克风支架。

speaker: Unknown
en: This part number, you can go to Amazon and get it.
zh: 这个型号，你可以去亚马逊买到它。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you don't know what microphone to get, probably shouldn't start on Amazon.
zh: 如果你不知道要买什么麦克风，可能不应该从亚马逊开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: Multiply that by many, many, many product categories.
zh: 将此乘以许多、许多、许多产品类别。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so what Amazon does is get you the SKU, but knowing what SKU you want is another job.
zh: 所以亚马逊做的是为你获取 SKU，但知道你想要什么 SKU 是另一项工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Claude Code can write you the code, but what code do you want?
zh: Claude Code 可以为你编写代码，但你想要什么代码？

speaker: Unknown
en: It can make you the features.
zh: 它可以为你制作功能。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sure, but what features do you want?
zh: 当然，但你想要什么功能？

speaker: Unknown
en: Who's your customer?
zh: 你的客户是谁？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's the right product for that customer?
zh: 对于那个客户来说什么是合适的产品？

speaker: Unknown
en: How are you going to take it to market?
zh: 你打算如何将其推向市场？

speaker: Unknown
en: And long way of answering a question, why do you hire McKinsey?
zh: 长话短说回答一个问题，你为什么要雇佣麦肯锡？

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you hiring them to get a 75-slide deck?
zh: 你雇佣他们是为了得到一份 75 页的幻灯片吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, narrowly, Claude cowork will make a really, really crappy version of that.
zh: 好吧，狭义地说，Claude cowork 会制作一个真正、真正蹩脚的版本。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you'll get all this kind of AI grifters on LinkedIn and Twitter and so on saying, "Hey, I made a McKinsey deck with Claude," and you look at it and you think, "Yeah, that's a bunch of doc crap."
zh: 你会在 LinkedIn 和 Twitter 等地方看到所有这些 AI 骗子说，“嘿，我用 Claude 制作了一个麦肯锡幻灯片，”你看了一眼心想，“是啊，那是一堆废话。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That's not what you'd get from McKinsey.
zh: 那不是你会从麦肯锡得到的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: But even if it was, that's not what you paid them for.
zh: 但即使是，那也不是你付钱给他们的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: What you actually pay Bain to do is to go and walk all over your company and work out.
zh: 你实际上付钱给 Bain 去做的是走遍你的整个公司并弄清楚。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes, but why is it that you didn't do that?
zh: 是的，但是为什么是你没有做到那一点呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: And how do the politics of this work?
zh: 以及这里的政治是如何运作的？

speaker: Unknown
en: And what do you actually need to do?
zh: 以及你实际上需要做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And let's go and talk to your customers and work out what they actually think, as opposed to what's on the first page of Google.
zh: 让我们去和你的客户谈谈，弄清楚他们的真实想法，而不是谷歌第一页上的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's all the other stuff.
zh: 所有其他的东西才是重点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the PowerPoint is just like the task, but that's not what you hired them for.
zh: 而 PowerPoint 只是那个任务，但这并不是你雇佣他们的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: The same with Amazon versus the retailer, the same with software development.
zh: 亚马逊与零售商的情况相同，软件开发也是如此。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you've got that kind of split.
zh: 所以你有了那种分类。

speaker: Unknown
en: The other analogy that occurred to me here is looking at the sort of class of industry that got steamrolled by the internet, because they had those two things and you could split the part.
zh: 这里的另一个类比是看看那类被互联网彻底摧毁的行业，因为他们有那两样东西，你可以把这部分分开。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you had the physical manufacturing or physical distribution, and then you had the other, the thing, what was the actual thing?
zh: 所以你有实体制造或实体分销，然后你有另一个，那个东西，实际的东西是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Classic examples would be newspapers and recorded music.
zh: 经典的例子是报纸和录制的音乐。

speaker: Unknown
en: So record companies do not think of themselves as being in the business of manufacturing small pieces of plastic, but that was what they actually did.
zh: 所以唱片公司不认为自己是从事制造小块塑料的业务的，但这实际上就是他们所做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when that went away, they were screwed.
zh: 当那个东西消失了，他们就完蛋了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Same thing with newspapers.
zh: 报纸也是一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: Newspapers did not think of themselves as manufacturing.
zh: 报纸不认为自己是制造业。

speaker: Unknown
en: And trucking companies.
zh: 还有卡车运输公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you decouple that, then that becomes a problem.
zh: 当你把那两者解耦时，那就会成为一个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: But often you can't decouple that, or that wasn't really the problem; or you make that thing cheap and then all this other stuff happens as well.
zh: 但通常你无法解耦那两者，或者那并不是真正的问题；或者你把那个东西变便宜了，然后所有其他这些事情也发生了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so all of this is just vastly more complicated than saying, "Well, hey, we're just going to automate the accountants or we're going to automate the consultants."
zh: 所以这一切都远比说，“好吧，嘿，我们只是要自动化会计师或者我们要自动化顾问”要复杂得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, there's two charts in the presentation of the number of people employed as accountants, which went up right the way through the 20th century, and has gone up again since the beginning of the 21st century.
zh: 我的意思是，演示文稿中有两张图表，显示了受雇为会计师的人数，这个数字在整个 20 世纪一直上升，并且自 21 世纪初以来再次上升。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you have adding machines, and punch cards, and mainframes, and databases, and ERP, and cloud, with spreadsheets and PCs, and the number of accountants keep going up.
zh: 所以你有了加法机、穿孔卡片、大型机、数据库、ERP，还有云端、电子表格和 PC，而会计师的数量一直在上升。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so why is that?
zh: 那么这是为什么呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, it's more complicated than automation.
zh: 嗯，这比自动化更复杂。

speaker: Unknown
en: Even just looking at the most advanced AI companies, Anthropic, OpenAI, I just had Dan Shipper from Every on the podcast, everyone's just increasing headcount.
zh: 即使只看最先进的 AI 公司，Anthropic，OpenAI，我刚刚请了来自 Every 的 Dan Shipper 上播客，每个人都在增加人手。

speaker: Unknown
en: The companies you would think would be least likely to add humans are adding many, many humans.
zh: 你认为最不可能增加人员的公司正在增加许许多多的人员。

speaker: Unknown
en: And since your point, it's really complicated.
zh: 鉴于你的观点，这真的很复杂。

speaker: Unknown
en: What you're just kind just on the job, the coming job apocalypse, like Dario's talking about all the entry level people are no more jobs, just like...
zh: 你刚才只是在那个工作上，关于即将到来的工作岗位末日，就像 Dario 说的那样，所有入门级人员再也没有工作了，就像……

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, there's a narrow point here, which is that I would place...
zh: 我的意思是，这里有一个狭隘的观点，那就是我会把……

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't like argument from authority.
zh: 我不喜欢诉诸权威的论证。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I don't think the fact that you run AI lab suddenly gives you... Or rather, if you're going to use argument from authority, then it should be relevant to the field.
zh: 而且我认为你经营 AI 实验室这一事实并不能突然给你…… 或者更确切地说，如果你要使用诉诸权威的论证，那么它应该与该领域相关。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, I'm interested in Dario's opinions on where models are going to go in the next six to 12 months.
zh: 所以，我对 Dario 关于模型在未来 6 到 12 个月的发展方向的观点感兴趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm not particularly interested in it opinions on series of labor and market value and comparative advantage.
zh: 我对他关于劳动力系列和市场价值以及比较优势的观点并不特别感兴趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, maybe he had a course on that at university, so did I.
zh: 是的，也许他在大学上过这门课，我也是。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, I think one needs to be a little bit cautious on what Dario says.
zh: 所以，我认为人们对 Dario 的话需要稍微谨慎一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's setting aside the cynical view that he's just doing that department stock, which I don't believe at all.
zh: 这撇开了那种愤世嫉俗的观点，即他只是在做那个部门股票，这一点我完全不相信。

speaker: Unknown
en: So this kind of comes back to my point about platform shifts.
zh: 所以这又回到了我关于平台转变的观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every time we have a new technology, it automates away a bunch of jobs.
zh: 每次我们有一项新技术，它都会自动化掉一堆工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then that automation, whether it's price elasticity, and the enablement of the fact that they became automated, unlock so much of new jobs.
zh: 然后那种自动化，无论是通过价格弹性，还是因为它们变得自动化这一事实所带来的赋能，解锁了如此多的新工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so you go back to 1800, 90% of us were peasants, and our major concern was like, are the crops going to fail?
zh: 所以你回到 1800 年，我们 90% 的人是农民，我们的主要担忧是，作物会歉收吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Because then we'll all go hungry.
zh: 因为那样我们都会挨饿。

speaker: Unknown
en: Or worse.
zh: 或者更糟。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so ever since then, we've been automating jobs and creating new jobs, and you can always see the job that's going to go away, and you don't know the new job because it doesn't exist yet, and it's like something that sounds dumb anyway.
zh: 从那时起，我们一直在自动化工作并创造新工作，你总是能看到将要消失的工作，而不知道那个新工作，因为它还不存在，而且听起来反正很蠢。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like railway engineer, what's a railway?
zh: 就像铁路工程师，什么是铁路？

speaker: Unknown
en: Why would that be a thing?
zh: 为什么那会是个东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: Who would want to go that fast?
zh: 谁想那么快地走？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we've had that process over and over again.
zh: 所以我们一次又一次地经历了这个过程。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is what any first-year economic student would tell you.

zh: 自 1800 年以来，我们一次又一次地经历了这个过程。

zh: 每次你经历它，你都会遇到一堆摩擦性的痛苦和错位，一群人做他们的工作，一群城镇被掏空，这一切都很糟。

speaker: Unknown
en: But when you come through on the other side, we're all richer, and we're not worried about the crops failing anymore.
zh: 但当你从另一边走出来时，我们所有人都更富有，我们不再担心作物歉收了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this is the process of the last 200 years.
zh: 这是过去 200 年的过程。

speaker: Unknown
en: So then the question is, is there some a primary reason why this would be different to those?
zh: 那么问题来了，是否有一些根本原因导致这会与那些不同？

speaker: Unknown
en: Because the internet removed a bunch of jobs, PCs removed a bunch of jobs.
zh: 因为互联网移除了一堆工作，PC 移除了一堆工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: There aren't many people working as typesetters anymore.
zh: 现在没有多少人在做排版工作了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Or telephone operators, or typists.
zh: 或者电话接线员，或者打字员。

speaker: Unknown
en: The internet removed a bunch of jobs.
zh: 互联网移除了一堆工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And generally the jobs that go away are crap jobs, seen retrospectively, and the new job, the better, because GDP keeps going up.
zh: 而且通常消失的工作回顾起来都是糟糕的工作，而新工作更好，因为 GDP 一直在上升。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, is AI different?
zh: 那么，AI 有什么不同吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so then there's kind of a couple of answers to this.
zh: 对此有几种答案。

speaker: Unknown
en: One theory is, well, this is going to be way quicker.
zh: 一种理论是，好吧，这会快得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And certainly the adoption of AI is quicker than previous technologies, but this is kind of because you're turning on the shoulders of giants.
zh: 当然，AI 的采用比以前的技术更快，但这有点是因为你们站在巨人的肩膀上。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you don't need to wait for everyone to buy a piece of expensive hardware, to buy a phone or a PC, or wait for the telco to deploy broadband.
zh: 所以你不需要等待每个人都购买昂贵的硬件，购买手机或 PC，或者等待电信公司部署宽带。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's already there.
zh: 那已经存在了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So of course, ChatGPT can get 900 million chat users, because there's already 900 million people on the internet.
zh: 所以当然，ChatGPT 可以获得 9 亿聊天用户，因为互联网上已经有 9 亿人了。

speaker: Unknown
en: When Mark Andreessen launched Netscape in what was it, '93, '94, there were like 50 to 100 million PCs on earth.
zh: 当 Mark Andreessen 在什么时候，93 年还是 94 年推出 Netscape 时，地球上大约有 5000 万到 1 亿台 PC。

speaker: Unknown
en: So now you didn't have 900 million users then.
zh: 所以那时你没有 9 亿用户。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the point is then he didn't need to wait for phone networks or microchips.
zh: 但重点是，那时他不需要等待电话网络或微芯片。

speaker: Unknown
en: And before that, you didn't need to wait for electricity, and you didn't need to wait for mass production.
zh: 在那之前，你不需要等待电力，你也不需要等待大规模生产。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you're always standing on the shoulders of giants.
zh: 所以你总是站在巨人的肩膀上。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's always a compounding effect.
zh: 总有一种复合效应。

speaker: Unknown
en: So yeah, this is faster, but the internet was faster too.
zh: 所以是的，这更快，但互联网也更快。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the other answer to this, and this kind of comes back to the professional services point is, you talk to these doomers on Twitter, and they would act like every big company is going to buy ChatGPT tomorrow, and then in two weeks time, they'll fire all their staff, and these people are mourners.
zh: 我认为对此的另一个答案，这有点回到专业服务的观点，你在 Twitter 上和这些悲观主义者交谈，他们会表现得好像每个大公司明天都要购买 ChatGPT，然后在两周内解雇所有员工，这些人是哀悼者。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is one of many reasons why the doomers were mourns, but complete failure to understand the way the world works.
zh: 这是悲观主义者是哀悼者的众多原因之一，但完全未能理解世界运作的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was like the starting point why they then didn't understand anything else.
zh: 这就像是他们为什么无法理解其他任何事情的起点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Turn to a big company, enterprise software sales cycle, you'll know this better than me, enterprise software sales cycle is like 18 months, if you're lucky.
zh: 看看大公司，企业软件销售周期，你比我更清楚，企业软件销售周期大约是 18 个月，如果你运气好的话。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is always a problem.
zh: 这总是一个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: The enterprise sales cycle is shorter than the venture backed funding cycle.
zh: 企业销售周期短于风险投资支持的资金周期。

speaker: Unknown
en: Longer rather.
zh: 应该说是更长。

speaker: Unknown
en: Longer.
zh: 更长。

speaker: Unknown
en: It takes you longer to get an enterprise deal than it takes you to go between raps.
zh: 完成一笔企业交易所需的时间比你轮次之间的时间还要长。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this was always supporting the building, particularly for sectors like aerospace, or healthcare, or something.
zh: 这总是支持建设，特别是对于航空航天或医疗保健等行业。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, I know people aren't just going to tear out SAP and replace it with X, Y, Z.
zh: 所以，我知道人们不会只是把 SAP 撕掉并换成 X、Y、Z。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe in like three, five, 10 years, yes, that whole estate will look radically different, and all those jobs will have changed, but it will take two, three, four, five, 10 years, and it will take time sector by sector, and it will take time for people to work out, "You could do that thing with this."
zh: 也许在三年、五年、十年后，是的，整个资产组合看起来会完全不同，所有这些工作都会改变，但这需要两年、三年、四年、五年、十年，并且需要时间一个行业接一个行业地进行，人们也需要时间弄清楚，“你可以用这个东西来做那件事。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And one of the companies I always remember that we looked at when I was at Andreessen Horowitz is a company called Frame.io, which is video editing, video collaboration.
zh: 我总是记得的一家公司是我们在 Andreessen Horowitz 时研究过的一家叫 Frame.io 的公司，它是视频编辑、视频协作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's nothing there that you couldn't have done at least five years earlier, and maybe 10 years earlier.
zh: 那里没有什么你至少早五年，也许早十年做不到的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And actually, that's kind of a bad example because that relies on a bunch of stuff like cutting edge web technologies.
zh: 实际上，这有点像是一个糟糕的例子，因为它依赖于一些前沿的网络技术。

speaker: Unknown
en: But if you go out and pick 10 random SaaS companies that were started the day before ChatGPT launched, how many of them could have been founded at any point in the previous 15 years?
zh: 但如果你出去挑选 10 家在 ChatGPT 推出的前一天成立的随机 SaaS 公司，它们中有多少是在前 15 年的任何时候都可以成立的？

speaker: Unknown
en: The delay was somebody realizing, "Oh, that problem exists inside that industry, and this is the way that we would solve it."
zh: 延迟是因为有人意识到，“哦，那个问题存在于那个行业内部，而这就是我们要解决它的方式。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It didn't all happen the day after Google Docs.
zh: 这并不是在 Google Docs 出现后的第二天就发生的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It took like 10, 15, 20 years for people to invent all that stuff, and work out that you could do that with this.
zh: 人们花了 10 年、15 年、20 年的时间来发明所有这些东西，并弄清楚可以用这个来做那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so all of that is like, the way of saying, well, yes, it is going to be quick, but actually, no, it will kind of take a while for people to work out how to completely change how their business works.
zh: 所以所有这些都像是，想表达的是，好吧，是的，它会很快，但实际上，不，人们弄清楚如何彻底改变他们的业务运作方式需要一段时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: Your view is so comforting, because basically it's like, okay, this is a huge deal, but we've been through many transformations before, and it's going to be okay.
zh: 你的观点令人欣慰，因为基本上就像是，好吧，这是一件大事，但我们以前经历过多次转型，而且一切都会好的。

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: So you spend two weeks to make the chart and then you look at it and go, "Yeah, I'm not going to use that."
zh: 所以你花了两周时间制作图表,然后你看着它说,"是的,我不打算用这个。"

speaker: Unknown
en: And for me, this was like two hours in Google.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: So we're all just kind of vibes forecasting as to what will happen.

en: "Maybe we aren't conscious either.

en: We just think we are."

en: "Yeah, great.

en: Thank you."

speaker: Unknown
en: And so certainly, I mean, I did do a poll on social media every now and then asking, "Is machine learning still AI?"

speaker: Unknown
en: Because I've certainly heard people say, "Well, that's not AI.

speaker: Unknown
en: That's just image recognition" "That's not AI.
zh: 那只是图像识别" "那不是 AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's just sentiment analysis."
zh: 那只是情感分析。"

en: So AI, it's a bit like the word technology.

en: It's like if it's new, then it's technology.

en: So is AEI, what's the definition now?

en: Well, that's a very different thing too.

speaker: Unknown
en: It has a soul and it's fucking alive.

speaker: Unknown

speaker: Unknown
en: It's funny, I was having an argument on Hacker News this morning, you remember the argument, which is never a good use of time, but you remember the argument of like people would argue about whether crypto is blockchain, or whether blockchain is crypto.
zh: 有趣的是，我今天早上在 Hacker News 上争论来着，你记得那个争论，那绝不是利用时间的好方式，但你记得那个争论，就像人们会争论加密货币是区块链，还是区块链是加密货币。

speaker: Unknown
en: There isn't a right answer to that.
zh: 那个问题没有正确答案。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let's just be sure.
zh: 我们还是确定一点吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's important to understand what you mean when you say that, but there isn't like a correct answer to this.
zh: 当你说那个时理解你的意思很重要，但这并没有像正确答案一样的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Are we going to get to something that has human level intelligence?
zh: 我们会得到某种具有人类水平智能的东西吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: We don't know.
zh: 我们不知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think we have any way of answering that question.
zh: 我认为我们没有任何办法回答那个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe, maybe not.
zh: 也许会，也许不会。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can make arguments either way.
zh: 你可以正反两面地争论。

speaker: Unknown
en: Meantime, in the meanwhile, we've got this thing that's clearly kind of a completely transformative technology.
zh: 与此同时，在这期间，我们有了这个东西，它显然是一种完全变革性的技术。

speaker: Unknown
en: And maybe the serious point here is like you don't have to believe, even if the model stopped it getting better tomorrow.
zh: 也许这里的严肃一点是，你不必相信，即使模型明天停止变好。

speaker: Unknown
en: If this is it and we hit a brick wall tomorrow, this is an incredibly useful technology that's going to change your world and get rolled out over the next 10 years.
zh: 如果就是这样，我们明天撞上南墙，这也是一项极其有用的技术，将在未来 10 年改变你的世界并推出。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you don't have to believe in any of that stuff to believe that this is a giant deal.
zh: 所以你不必相信任何那些东西也能相信这是一件大事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something that's definitely changed, I had your former boss, Mark Andreessen on the podcast, and we didn't actually talk about this during the conversation, and he brought it up before we started recording and I never got to it, is he had this insight that the opportunity set for companies now is so much larger.
zh: 有一件事肯定改变了，我请过你前任老板 Mark Andreessen 上播客，我们在谈话中实际上没有谈论这个，他在开始录音前提起了这个，我从来没讲到它，他有这个洞察，即现在公司的机会集要大得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: We used to have no trillion-dollar companies, now we're going to have dozens of trillion-dollar companies.
zh: 我们过去没有万亿美元的公司，现在我们要有几十家万亿美元的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just like the size companies can grow to are going up so much, and valuations also go up along with that.
zh: 就像公司可以成长的规模正在大幅上升，估值也随之上升。

speaker: Unknown
en: And his point is just people haven't really grokked just how large companies can get now.
zh: 他的观点是人们还没有真正意识到现在公司可以变得多大。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everyone's hitting a hundred million AR in five months, six months.
zh: 每个人都在五六个月内达到一亿 AR。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just thoughts on that.
zh: 就这点想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, this was his whole software's eating the world thesis from 15 years ago, whenever it was.
zh: 我的意思是，这是他 15 年前的「软件吞噬世界」论点，不管那是什​​时候。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: The TAM, it gets progressively bigger, because you can address larger and larger parts of the economy.
zh: TAM（潜在市场总额），它会越来越大，因为你可以解决经济中越来越多的部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so if you think about the classic platform ship framing that mainframes are, I think peak mainframe install base was something like 70, 80,000 units.
zh: 所以如果你考虑经典的平台轮次框架，大型机是，我认为大型机安装基础的峰值大约是 7 万到 8 万台。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, slightly fuzzy term, what exactly is a mainframe, and at what point does it become two mainframes as one.
zh: 我的意思是，稍微有点模糊的术语，确切地说什么是大型机，在什么点它变成两台大型机算作一台。

speaker: Unknown
en: But something like that, that order of magnitude.
zh: 但大概是那样，那个数量级。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then when the internet kicks off, there are, as I said, 50 to 100 million PCs on earth, maybe.
zh: 然后当互联网起飞时，有，正如我所说，地球上有 5000 万到 1 亿台 PC，也许。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today there are something over a billion, one to one and a half billion, but obviously a lot of those are corporate.
zh: 现在有超过 10 亿台，10 到 15 亿台，但显然其中很多是企业的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like seven, 800 million consumer PCs in the world.
zh: 就像世界上有 7 亿到 8 亿台消费级 PC。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's about five and a half, six billion smartphones in the world, which is why you can have 900 million weekly active users on ChatGPT.
zh: 世界上大约有 55 亿到 60 亿部智能手机，这就是为什么你在 ChatGPT 上可以有 9 亿周活跃用户。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so there was this narrative five years ago of like, "Well, we've run out of people.
zh: 所以五年前有这种叙事，“嗯，我们用完了人。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the next thing can't be in order of magnitude bigger."
zh: 所以下一个东西不可能大一个数量级。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Which was true up to a point, but that was the wrong model, because clearly what's happening now as you're moving in another direction, is you're just branching out and automating big news weights of the economy.
zh: 这在某种程度上是真的，但那是错误的模型，因为显然现在发生的事情是你正朝另一个方向移动，你只是正在分叉出去并将经济中的重要权重自动化。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, back to your job point, you could argue, well, we're just going to replace all the people with AI and all the money will go to Sam Altman.
zh: 现在，回到你关于工作的观点，你可能会争辩说，好吧，我们要用 AI 替换所有人，所有的钱都会给 Sam Altman。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Mark can buy himself another Gulfstream, add to the fleet.
zh: Mark 可以给自己再买一架湾流飞机，加入机队。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the other answer, it's back to the lump of labor fallacy, and the last 200 years, that each of these technologies removes a bunch of jobs, creates a bunch of new jobs, creates a bunch of new value, unlocks prosperity for all of us, and that's painful as you go through it, but it always creates more value.
zh: 我认为另一个答案，这又回到了劳动总量谬误，在过去 200 年里，这些技术中的每一项都消除了一堆工作，创造了一堆新工作，创造了一堆新价值，为我们所有人解锁了繁荣，当你经历它时这很痛苦，但它总是创造更多价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so here you could certainly make an analog to...
zh: 所以在这里你当然可以做一个类比……

speaker: Unknown
en: The useful analog to the electricity industry is just saying how that electricity became part of absolutely everything.
zh: 对电力行业有用的类比只是说电力如何成为绝对一切的一部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: And software has been kind of slowly working its way out.
zh: 而软件一直有点慢慢地在向外发展。

speaker: Unknown
en: Another would be electricity in factories, and then electricity sort of slowly spreads out.
zh: 另一个是工厂里的电力，然后电力慢慢传播出去。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that would be the point again, that it slowly spreads out to do more and more things.
zh: 所以这也是重点，它慢慢传播出去做越来越多的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so more and more value and a bigger and bigger contribution to the economy.
zh: 所以越来越多的价值和对经济越来越大的贡献。

speaker: Unknown
en: Also, of course, disappears inside things.
zh: 当然，也消失在事物内部。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the other side, the point of my capital section in the presentation is there's this quote from Sam Altman where he said, "We're going to be selling AI intelligence on a meter like water or electricity."
zh: 另一方面，我在演示文稿中的资本部分的观点是 Sam Altman 有这样一句话，“我们将像水或电一样通过仪表销售 AI 智能。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And you look at this and think, "My dear sweet child, you need me to explain the margin structure of the utility industry to you."
zh: 你看着这个想，“我亲爱的孩子，你需要我给你解释公用事业行业的利润率结构吗。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Because guess what?
zh: 因为猜猜看？

speaker: Unknown
en: When you watch television, the TV company isn't paying a percentage of your monthly bill to the electricity company.
zh: 当你看电视时，电视公司并不是把你的月度账单的一定百分比付给电力公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you wash your clothes, Bosch isn't paying a percentage of the price of the washing machine.
zh: 当你洗衣服时，博世并不是把洗衣机价格的一定百分比付出去。

speaker: Unknown
en: And clearly, this is like the much more specific tactical question at the moment, is, do we even end up with three giant models or does it become hundreds of models and open models and local models and so on?
zh: 显然，这就像目前更加具体的战术问题，我们最终真的会得到三个巨型模型吗？还是它会变成数百个模型和开源模型和本地模型等等？

speaker: Unknown
en: And even if we do end up with, say, pick a number, three to six to 10 giant foundation models that cost hundreds of billions of dollars a year, fine, do they get all the value from that?
zh: 即使我们最终确实得到了，比如说，选个数字，三到六个到十个巨型基础模型，每年花费数千亿美元，好吧，它们会从中获得所有价值吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, I started my career as a telecom analyst, and so still pay attention to it a bit.
zh: 现在，我的职业生涯是从电信分析师开始的，所以仍然有点关注它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Global mobile industry has revenue of about a trillion dollars a year, maybe a bit more now.
zh: 全球移动行业的年收入约为 1 万亿美元，现在可能多一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it spends about $200 billion a year on CapEx every year.
zh: 它每年在资本支出上花费约 2000 亿美元。

speaker: Unknown
en: Total telecoms is about 300.
zh: 总电信业约为 3000 亿。

speaker: Unknown
en: Mobile is about 200.
zh: 移动业务约为 2000 亿。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's about 15, 20% of revenue every year.
zh: 这大约是每年收入的 15% 到 20%。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you look at a chart of mobile data consumption, it's an exponential curve, like perfect curve going spread up.
zh: 如果你查看移动数据消耗量的图表，它是一条指数曲线，就像完美的曲线向上升。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the number now, I think it's about 1,500 to 2,000 times what it was in 2010 globally.
zh: 这个数字现在，我认为它大约是 2010 年全球水平的 1500 到 2000 倍。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the stocks have gone nowhere in 25 years, because it's an X gross low margin commodity utility, where they're selling this objectively amazing piece of global technology infrastructure that has enormous complexity and enormous sophistication, but all the cool stuff is made by you.
zh: 而这些股票在 25 年里哪里也没去，因为它是一个 X 毛利率低利润的大宗商品公用事业，他们在销售这种客观上令人惊叹的全球技术基础设施，它具有巨大的复杂性和巨大的精密性，但所有很酷的东西都是你制造的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's made by the people listening to this podcast.
zh: 它是由这个播客的听众制造的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's made by somebody else.
zh: 它是由别人制造的。

speaker: Unknown
en: This was that kind of pivotal moment where the telcos thought that they would do all the stuff that you did on your iPhone.
zh: 那就是那种关键时刻，电信运营商以为他们会做你在 iPhone 上做的所有事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And not only do they not do it, but Apple doesn't do it either.
zh: 而且不仅他们没有做，苹果也没有做。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's all further up stack.
zh: 这一切都进一步深入堆栈。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so this is the elemental question right now around foundation models is, does the model do the whole thing?
zh: 所以这就是目前围绕基础模型的要素问题，模型会做整个事情吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you just go to the chatbot and get the chatbot to do the whole thing?
zh: 你只是去聊天机器人那里并让聊天机器人做整个事情吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Can the model companies keep building these like Claude for X, Claude for Y things, which to me look very much like what you see if you hit file new in Excel.
zh: 模型公司能继续构建这些像用于 X 的 Claude，用于 Y 的 Claude 的东西吗，这在我看来就像你在 Excel 中点击文件新建时所看到的一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like the tablets, but all of those are actually billion-dollar companies as well.
zh: 它就像那些平板电脑，但所有那些实际上也是十亿美元级别的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if not, no, does it all have to be "apps"?
zh: 如果不是，不，这一切是否都必须是「应用」？

speaker: Unknown
en: Whatever app means.
zh: 不管应用意味着什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if it all has to be apps who builds those, well, they can't all get built by the model labs, just as they didn't all get built by Microsoft.
zh: 如果这一切都必须是应用，谁构建那些，好吧，它们不能全部由模型实验室构建，就像它们没有全部由微软构建一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so if they're all built by other companies, does their notion models have leverage up the stack the way Windows did?
zh: 而且如果它们都由其他公司构建，他们那种模型公司的概念是否像 Windows 那样在堆栈中拥有杠杆作用？

speaker: Unknown
en: Or is this more like AWS where, if you're, I don't know, an engineering company or a law firm buying a piece of software, you don't care which cloud it runs on?
zh: 还是这更像 AWS，在那里，如果你，我不知道，是一家工程公司或一家购买软件的律师事务所，你不在乎它运行在哪个云上？

speaker: Unknown
en: And you don't have to standardize on AWS because that's where all the software is.
zh: 而且你不必标准化使用 AWS，因为那是所有软件所在的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the developers all standardize on AWS because all the customers use AWS.
zh: 而且开发者都标准化使用 AWS，因为所有客户都使用 AWS。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's not how it works.
zh: 那不是它的工作方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's how Windows or iOS works, but that's not how AWS works.
zh: 那是 Windows 或 iOS 的工作方式，但这不是 AWS 的工作方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it does sort of seem to me that if the chatbot isn't the UX, and it needs to be apps, and the model companies aren't going to build that, and the models themselves are basically commodities, at least as you can see them as users, then why would the model companies have pricing power?
zh: 所以对我来说确实似乎是，如果聊天机器人不是 UX，它需要是应用，并且模型公司不会构建那个，并且模型本身基本上是大宗商品，至少正如你作为用户看到的那样，那么为什么模型公司会有定价权？

speaker: Unknown
en: And wouldn't all the value be further up the stack?
zh: 并且难道所有价值不会进一步深入堆栈吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Aren't you basically, have you got like three to six companies selling a commodity at marginal cost?
zh: 你是否基本上有，你是否得到了像三到六家公司以边际成本销售大宗商品？

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, obviously the semi-analyst guys are like, "No, no, no, no, no.
zh: 现在，显然半导体分析师们就像，「不，不，不，不，不。」

speaker: Unknown
en: There's going to be infinite pricing power forever."
zh: 永远会有无限的定价权。」

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm sorry, I'm exaggerating.
zh: 抱歉，我夸大了。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think you have to really important to draw a distinction between where are we now, where you have radical price disequilibrium, and you've got these, what's the guy, the OpenClaw guy spent one and a half million dollars on token this last month.
zh: 但我认为你必须真的很重要的一点是区分我们现在在哪里，你那里有激进的价格不平衡，并且你得到了这些，那是谁，那个 OpenClaw 家伙上个月在 token 上花了 150 万美元。

speaker: Unknown
en: But that's somebody getting like a 50 grand mobile data built in 2010.
zh: 但那是某人在 2010 年得到大约 5 万美元的移动数据一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's temporary.
zh: 那是暂时的。

speaker: Unknown
en: What is the steady state equilibrium point where all of these lines on the chart kind of get lined up, and we don't have this kind of weird, crazy stuff going on?
zh: 稳态平衡点是什么，图表上的所有这些线基本上对齐了，而我们没有这种奇怪的、疯狂的事情发生？

speaker: Unknown
en: And then will you have pricing power, or have you got like three or four or five companies kind of all selling the same thing?
zh: 然后你会拥有定价权吗，还是你有像三四个或五个公司都在销售同一种东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so then you should have a pricing price.
zh: 那么你应该有一个定价价格。

speaker: Unknown
en: You should have lower pricing and lower margins, and the value you've got stack.
zh: 你应该拥有更低的定价和更低的利润率，以及你拥有的堆栈价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: I am so excited to tell you about this season's supporting sponsor Vanta.
zh: 我非常激动地告诉你关于这一季度的支持赞助商 Vanta。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vanta helps over 15,000 companies like Cursor, Ramp, Duolingo, Snowflake, and Atlassian.
zh: Vanta 帮助超过 15,000 家公司，像 Cursor、Ramp、Duolingo、Snowflake 和 Atlassian。

speaker: Unknown
en: Earn and prove trust with their customers.
zh: 赢得并证明他们客户的信任。

speaker: Unknown
en: Teams are building and shipping products faster than ever thanks to AI.
zh: 团队正在以前所未有的速度构建和交付产品，这要归功于 AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: But as a result, the amount of risk being introduced into your product and your business is higher than it's ever been.
zh: 但结果是，引入到你的产品和业务中的风险量比以往任何时候都高。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every security leader that I talk to is feeling the increasing weight of protecting their organization, their business, and not to mention their customer data.
zh: 我交谈过的每一位安全领导者都感受到了保护其组织、其业务的压力，更不用说他们的客户数据了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because things are moving so fast, they are constantly reacting, having to guess at priorities, and having to make do with outdated solutions.
zh: 因为事情发展得太快，他们不断在做出反应，不得不猜测优先级，并将就使用过时的解决方案。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vanta automates compliance and risk management, with over 35 security and privacy frameworks, including SOC 2, ISO 27,001 and HIPAA.
zh: Vanta 自动化合规和风险管理，涵盖超过 35 种安全和隐私框架，包括 SOC 2、ISO 27,001 和 HIPAA。

speaker: Unknown
en: This helps companies get compliant fast and stay compliant.
zh: 这帮助公司快速合规并保持合规。

speaker: Unknown
en: More than ever before, trust has the power to make or break your business.
zh: 与以往任何时候相比，信任更有能力成就或毁掉你的业务。

speaker: Unknown
en: Learn more at vanta.com/lenny.
zh: 了解更多信息请访问 vanta.com/lenny。

speaker: Unknown
en: And as a listener of this podcast, you get $1,000 off Vanta.
zh: 作为本播客的听众，您可以获得 Vanta 1000 美元的优惠。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's vanta.com/lenny.
zh: 那就是 vanta.com/lenny。

speaker: Unknown
en: A really interesting takeaway here is that your sense is over time the foundational model companies, Anthropic, OpenAI, others, their margins will get squeezed, they will not be as successful as they are today, and the bigger opportunities in the application layer, the people building on the model, the wrappers.
zh: 这里一个非常有趣的结论是，你的感觉是随着时间的推移，基础模型公司，Anthropic、OpenAI 和其他公司，他们的利润率会受到挤压，他们不会像今天这样成功，而更大的机会在应用层，那些在模型之上构建的人，那些包装器。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, this is a very sort of deterministic thesis, which is, the models companies, crucially what I said is the models don't seem to have no after effects.
zh: 我的意思是，这是一个非常确定性的论点，也就是说，模型公司，关键是我说过模型似乎没有什么后效。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there doesn't seem to be a winner takes all effect where one of these will run away ahead of the other.
zh: 所以似乎不存在那种赢家通吃的效应，让其中一个遥遥领先于其他公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you should have competition indefinitely.
zh: 所以你应该拥有无限的竞争。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you have competition indefinitely, you don't have primary really radical differentiation of what the product is.
zh: 如果你有无限的竞争，你实际上就没有产品本质上的根本差异化。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then why would you have pricing power?
zh: 那么你为什么会有定价权呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: And meanwhile, if you need to have thousands of applications that are all different built by different people, those can't all be built by the model people.
zh: 同时，如果你需要数千个不同的应用程序，由不同的人构建，这些不可能都由模型公司的人来构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, it should end up looking more like cloud than it looks like Windows.
zh: 所以，它最终应该看起来更像云，而不是像 Windows。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now that may be completely wrong, and one of the points I make in the presentation is like, imagine having this conversation about the internet in 1997, what would you have got right?
zh: 现在这可能完全错了，我在演示中提出的观点之一是，想象一下在 1997 年进行关于互联网的对话，你会说对什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Or indeed having it about mobile in 2000.
zh: 或者确实在 2000 年进行关于移动设备的对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: You would've missed almost all of it.
zh: 你几乎会错过所有这一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: You certainly would've said that a has-been PC company from [inaudible 00:39:28] would win the whole thing.
zh: 你肯定会说一家来自 [听不清 00:39:28] 的没落的 PC 公司会赢得整场游戏。

speaker: Unknown
en: And no one would've said that.
zh: 没有人会那样说。

speaker: Unknown
en: And a search company with a weird logo like, "Search, what's that got to do with mobile?"
zh: 还有一家有着奇怪标志的搜索公司，比如，“搜索，那跟移动设备有什么关系？”

speaker: Unknown
en: "No, forget it, you're an idiot."
zh: “不，算了，你是个白痴。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So we should presume we don't know, but they're all this sort of basic building blocks of like, well, but why would they have pricing power?
zh: 所以我们应该假设我们不知道，但它们都是这些基本构建块，比如，好吧，但为什么它们会有定价权？

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know, when I was a baby analyst in '99, went to see a dotcom company in the UK that was trying to do online selling computer cars, components online.
zh: 我不知道，当我在 99 年还是一名初级分析师时，去英国看一家 dotcom 公司，这家公司试图在线做在线销售计算机汽车，组件在线。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they had this whole model and this whole story in the brand and buy the whole thing and we went up to see them, and we're on the train back from Birmingham, and this just senior banker called David Tate.
zh: 他们有这整个模型和这整个故事以及品牌，购买整个东西，我们去看他们，我们在从伯明翰回来的火车上，这个高级银行家叫 David Tate。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're all sitting talking about it, and Tate says, "It's a low margin reseller, one time sells."
zh: 我们都坐着谈论这件事，Tate 说，“这是一个低利润率的转售商，一次性销售。”

speaker: Unknown
en: "You can say dotcom all you like, it's a low margin reseller."
zh: “你可以随你怎么说 dotcom，它就是一个低利润率的转售商。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's the crux of this, is they're undifferentiated commodity infrastructure providers.
zh: 我认为这就是关键，它们是无差异化的商品基础设施提供商。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a lot of science to it, but there's a lot of science in mobile.
zh: 这里面有很多科学，但移动设备里也有很多科学。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, what do you pay for flat panel screen?
zh: 我的意思是，你为平板屏幕付多少钱？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's Nobel Prizes in flat panel screens.
zh: 平板屏幕有诺贝尔奖。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's still a low margin commodity.
zh: 它仍然是一个低利润率的商品。

speaker: Unknown
en: I look forward to be proving wrong, proven wrong, but like, hey, that's what it looks like now.
zh: 我期待被证明是错的，被证明是错的，但是，嘿，这就是它现在的样子。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is great.
zh: 这很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I know you're not an investor.
zh: 所以我知道你不是投资者。

speaker: Unknown
en: I know you didn't actually do investing at A16C even though you work for A16Z.
zh: 我知道你实际上没有在 A16C 做投资，即使你为 A16Z 工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Partner.
zh: 合伙人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Partner.
zh: 合伙人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just sit around and pontificate partner.
zh: 只是坐着空谈的合伙人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Are there companies you would invest in?
zh: 有没有你会投资的公司？

speaker: Unknown
en: If there are a couple companies you'd invest in now, is there some on that list, or categories even?
zh: 如果有几家你现在会投资的公司，名单上有吗，或者是类别？

speaker: Unknown
en: I mentioned briefly that I was an analyst.
zh: 我简短地提到过我是一名分析师。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was a sell side equity analyst.
zh: 我是一名卖方股票分析师。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was not a very good sell side equity analyst, but partly because I was not interested in talking to clients, partly because I was not interested in share prices, which would seem to be like a disqualification to be an equity analyst.
zh: 我不是一名很好的卖方股票分析师，但部分是因为我不喜欢跟客户谈话，部分是因为我对股价不感兴趣，这似乎像是取消作为股票分析师的资格。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a huge difference between being right and being early, and there's a huge difference between the right company and the right price.
zh: 在正确和提早正确之间有很大的区别，在正确的公司和正确的价格之间也有很大的区别。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, deterministically, you can look across the market and say, well, it's like the bell curve IQMium, and the guy with 50 and the guy with 200 are both saying, "Jeff Bezos, smart guy, I buy stock."
zh: 现在，确定性地说，你可以纵观市场并说，好吧，这就像智商的正态分布，智商 50 的人和智商 200 的人都在说，“Jeff Bezos，聪明人，我买股票。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And you can certainly overthink all of this.
zh: 你当然可以把这一切想得太复杂。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you can look at Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon, and say, "Hard to see a problem for them really with all of this."
zh: 你可以看 Google、Apple、Facebook、Amazon，并说，“真的很难看到它们有什么问题。”

speaker: Unknown
en: You can certainly see questions for all of them, and one of them may drop the ball, but it's worth remembering what happened in mobile.
zh: 你当然可以看到针对它们所有人的问题，其中之一可能会掉链子，但值得记住的是在移动端发生过的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: The internet was just like a big obvious platform shift.
zh: 互联网就像是一个巨大的、显而易见的平台转变。

speaker: Unknown
en: The funny thing about mobile is that some companies missed it completely, and for some of them it really didn't change anything.
zh: 关于移动端的一件有趣的事情是，有些公司完全错过了它，而对其中一些公司来说，这并没有改变任何事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: For Google, it didn't change anything.
zh: 对于 Google，这没有改变任何事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: For Meta, this was great.
zh: 对于 Meta，这很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is a way better way to do social than on PC, because you've got a camera, and notifications, and it's on your phone all the time with you.
zh: 这是一种比在 PC 上做社交好得多的方式，因为你有了相机，还有通知，而且它一直在你的手机上陪着你。

speaker: Unknown
en: Amazon, what does this change?
zh: Amazon，这改变了什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: It doesn't change everything.
zh: 它并没有改变一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, I'm massively oversimplifying here, but the point is now, meanwhile, Yahoo Mail fails to make the jump, there are companies that were already kind of dying that failed to make the jump, maybe eBay.
zh: 我是说，我在这里做了过度的简化，但重点是，与此同时，Yahoo Mail 未能实现跨越，有一些已经快死掉的公司未能实现跨越，也许是 eBay。

speaker: Unknown
en: You could argue about individual names.
zh: 你可以争论具体的名字。

speaker: Unknown
en: The point is that we went through that shift and it didn't change anything for half the industry.
zh: 重点是我们经历了那次转变，而这并没有改变半个行业的任何事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Half the internet industry.
zh: 半个互联网行业。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think that you could propose a little bit of that here.
zh: 所以我认为你可以在这里提出一点点类似的情况。

speaker: Unknown
en: While Steven Jay Sinofsky at A16Z who used to run Windows would always say, "Incumbents always try and make the new thing a feature."
zh: 虽然 A16Z 的 Steven Jay Sinofsky，他曾经负责 Windows，他总是会说，“在位者总是试图把新事物变成一个功能。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And sometimes they're right, sometimes it's a feature.
zh: 有时他们是对的，有时它就是一个功能。

speaker: Unknown
en: Actually, along those lines, something I wanted to get your take on.
zh: 实际上，顺着这些思路，有件事我想听听你的看法。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's this thread that's been happening across a bunch of guests, which is around distribution becoming a bigger and bigger moat, because as software is easier to build, everyone's launching products, everyone's trying to compete for attention, it's getting harder and harder.
zh: 有一个话题在一堆嘉宾中反复出现，就是关于分发正在变成越来越大的护城河，因为随着软件越来越容易构建，每个人都在发布产品，每个人都在争夺注意力，这变得越来越难。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's always been hard to get people's attention, but it's just like the noise in the market is just going up like crazy.
zh: 获取人们的注意力一直很难，但这就像市场中的噪音在疯狂地增加。

speaker: Unknown
en: And to me that tells me distribution is becoming a more and more valuable skill and asset.
zh: 对我来说，这告诉我分发正在变成一种越来越有价值的技能和资产。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it also tells me incumbents are going to be a lot more successful because they already have distribution versus a startup that's trying to break through.
zh: 这也告诉我，在位者将会成功得多，因为他们已经有了分发，相比于一个试图突围的初创公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, there's like a version of the Drake meme of like he says, "I don't like that.
zh: 我是说，这就像那个 Drake 梗图的版本，他说，“我不喜欢那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I do like this."
zh: 我喜欢这个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "I don't like GPT wrappers.
zh: 就像是，“我不喜欢 GPT wrappers。

speaker: Unknown
en: I do like harnesses."
zh: 我喜欢 harnesses。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So yeah, I did spend some time talking about this in the presentation I did at the end of last year, that if the product is a commodity, then the distribution is what matters.
zh: 所以是的，我确实在去年年底的演讲中花了一些时间谈论这个，如果产品是同质化商品，那么分发才是重要的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I wrote a thing about ChatGPT earlier this year, OpenAI earlier this year, how do they compete.
zh: 我今年早些时候写了一篇关于 ChatGPT，也就是 OpenAI 的文章，它们如何竞争。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, there's an obvious comparison that a lot of people made is with web browsers.
zh: 好的，有一个很多人做出的明显比较，就是和网络浏览器。

speaker: Unknown
en: The fundamentally web browser, and there's a distinction here I think between the web browser as product and web browser rendering engine, in that the rendering engine can be better or worse, but the browser product is just like a really thin wrapper for a rendering engine.
zh: 根本上的网络浏览器，我认为这里有一个区别，在于作为产品的网络浏览器和网络浏览器渲染引擎之间，渲染引擎可能有好有坏，但浏览器产品真的只是渲染引擎的一个非常薄的包装器。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's an input box and an output box.
zh: 有一个输入框和一个输出框。

speaker: Unknown
en: And like, what else?
zh: 还有，还有什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And which is like, what's the last innovation in browser design?
zh: 这就引出了，浏览器设计的最后一个创新是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Tab browsing, which was 20 years ago, 25 years ago.
zh: 标签页浏览，那是 20 年前，25 年前的事了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And every now and then somebody tries to innovate in browser design and it never works because you found the Platonic ideal.
zh: 偶尔有人尝试在浏览器设计上创新，但从未成功，因为你已经找到了柏拉图式的理想形态。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like trying to innovate in smartphone design.
zh: 这就像尝试在智能手机设计上创新一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a glass rate angle.
zh: 它就是一个玻璃的长方形。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's nothing you can do there.
zh: 你在那里无能为力。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so what happened, of course, is that Microsoft uses distribution to break in.
zh: 所以当然发生的事情是，Microsoft 利用分发强行进入。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then of course, what also happens is setting aside the lawsuit, is that it turns out the winning browsers doesn't matter anyway because the value is further up stack.
zh: 然后当然，另外发生的事情是，撇开诉讼不谈，结果证明获胜的浏览器并不重要，因为价值在堆栈的更高层。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so Microsoft, we use browsers for like five, six years, and it doesn't matter, and it doesn't get them anything.
zh: 所以 Microsoft，我们使用浏览器大概五六年，但这无关紧要，也没给他们带来任何东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: So clearly what's happening now is that Google is using distribution to drive Gemini.
zh: 所以很明显现在发生的是，Google 正在利用分发来推动 Gemini。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what's the difference between Gemini and Claude?
zh: Gemini 和 Claude 之间有什么区别？

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you're using this stuff all day, then you know, but like normal person, there's no difference.
zh: 如果你全天都在使用这些东西，那么你会知道，但对于普通人来说，没有区别。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the same thing with Meta, if you look at survey data on which our M people use, even before the news thing, like the Llama thing, Meta was behind, it was up there between ChatGPT and Gemini, which if you're in tech, people have completely written it off, but it was like they sprayed it on every service surface.
zh: Meta 也是一样，如果你看关于我们 M 哪些人使用的调查数据，甚至在新闻这件事，也就是 Llama 那件事之前，Meta 就落后了，它排在 ChatGPT 和 Gemini 之间，如果你在科技圈，人们已经完全把它排除在外了，但实际上就像是他们把它喷洒到了每一个服务表面上。

speaker: Unknown
en: It wasn't that bad.
zh: 它并不差。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was fine.
zh: 它挺好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So distribution of an adequate product when the field is basically commodity distribution on brand become a big deal, you could see that in the strategy, OpenAI strategy late last year was, people called it Everything, Everywhere, Yesterday.
zh: 所以当一个足够好的产品在一个基本上是同质化商品的领域里，分发和品牌就变成了一件大事，你可以从策略中看到这一点，OpenAI 去年年底的策略是，人们称之为 Everything, Everywhere, Yesterday。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so they were just trying everything to work out how they would get that.
zh: 所以他们只是在尝试一切，以弄清楚如何获得那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: How can we get a flyway or how can we get distribution?
zh: 我们如何获得一个航线，或者我们如何获得分发？

speaker: Unknown
en: How can we get something that sticks?
zh: 我们如何获得一些能留存下来的东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: How can we get something that people uses before Google, and Meta, and Amazon spray it everywhere and get everybody using that one?
zh: 我们怎样才能让人们使用某些东西，在 Google、Meta 和 Amazon 把它到处铺开并让每个人都使用那一个之前？

speaker: Unknown
en: And then you've got the inertia and the power of the default and like, why would you switch?
zh: 然后你就有了惯性和默认的力量，就像，你为什么要换呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: Obviously Apple is kind of the last penny to drop here.
zh: 显然苹果在这里是最后掉的一枚硬币。

speaker: Unknown
en: There was this sort of slightly weird opening idea, and now there's even weirder story that OpenAI want to sue Apple.
zh: 有这种稍微有点奇怪的开放想法，现在甚至更奇怪的故事说 OpenAI 想起诉苹果。

speaker: Unknown
en: Good luck with that.
zh: 祝他们好运。

speaker: Unknown
en: The funny thing about the Apple deal thing is just, not to go off on a tangent, but if you go back and watch the WWGC from 2024, the whole second half of it is Apple Intelligence.
zh: 关于苹果这件事的有趣之处在于，只是，不要跑题，如果你回去看 2024 年的 WWGC，整个下半部分都是 Apple Intelligence。

speaker: Unknown
en: That was like the most compelling vision of a personal AI assistant, still the most compelling vision I've seen.
zh: 那就像是我见过的关于个人 AI 助手的最引人注目的愿景，仍然是我见过的最引人注目的愿景。

speaker: Unknown
en: They then couldn't ship it, but then neither was anybody else.
zh: 然后他们没法发货它，但其他人也没能做到。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you watch it again and you're like, "Okay, so you want tool using a Agentic on device, AI with no prompt injection and no hallucinations, and a completely standardized API system across 10,000 apps with intents that all work perfectly."
zh: 你再看一遍你会想，“好吧，所以你想要工具使用，Agentic 在端侧的 AI，没有 prompt injection 和没有幻觉，以及一个跨 10,000 个应用的完全标准化的 API 系统，并且 intents 都完美工作。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm like, "Well, that sounds good to me, but I'm not surprised they couldn't ship it."
zh: 我就像，“嗯，对我来说听起来不错，但我并不惊讶他们没法发货它。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But nobody else would ship that.
zh: 但也没有其他人能发货那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: But that vision was great.
zh: 但那个愿景很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: I really want to see what happens at WWGC in a month.
zh: 我真的很想看一个月后的 WWGC 会发生什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do they actually ship that now, powered by Gemini?
zh: 他们现在真的发货那个了吗，由 Gemini 驱动？

speaker: Unknown
en: But that's also another point, is like, okay, there's going to be the AI intelligence, whatever we call, a Gemini intelligence on Android, and then there's going to be Apple intelligence on iOS, which is powered by Gemini, but it's not going to be the same set of products.
zh: 但这也是另一回事，就是，好吧，会有 AI 智能体，不管我们叫它什么，Android 上的 Gemini 智能体，然后是 iOS 上的 Apple 智能体，它由 Gemini 驱动，但这不会是同一套产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: The model's just like the dumb thing underneath the, funny way of putting it, the dumb thing underneath that power's a feature.
zh: 模型就像是在下面的那个笨东西，有趣的说法，是那个动力的功能特性下面的笨东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: The model is the commodity that powers different decisions about what the feature should be and what different distribution.
zh: 模型是商品，它为关于功能特性应该是什么以及不同分销的不同决策提供动力。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in that situation, of course, Apple's got a billion devices that can run this on edge.
zh: 而且在这种情况下，当然，苹果有十亿台设备可以在边缘运行这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Google has this wonderful marketing slogan coming soon to our most powerful devices, meaning it will work on those Androids.
zh: Google 有这句很棒的营销口号“即将登陆我们要最强大的设备”，意思是它将适用于那些 Android。

speaker: Unknown
en: So again, distribution questions.
zh: 所以又是分销问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Interesting.
zh: 有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: Google AO's next week, so we'll see what they launched.
zh: Google AO 在下周，所以我们会看他们发布什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh no, they launched the Android and it just shows how modules it-
zh: 哦不，他们发布了 Android 并且它只是展示了它有多模块——

speaker: Unknown
en: As it today?
zh: 就在今天？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, no, they launched it last week, which it just illustrates how much we start paying attention to Android and iPhone.
zh: 嗯，不，他们上周发布了它，这只是说明了我们开始关注 Android 和 iPhone 的程度有多低。

speaker: Unknown
en: Google did had a whole big thing last week.
zh: Google 上周确实搞了整个大动作。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're replacing Chromebooks with Google Books, and they've got a new Android intelligence powered by Gemini that will roll out to the five people who bought a Pixel phone.
zh: 他们正在用 Google Books 替换 Chromebooks，并且他们有了一个新的由 Gemini 驱动的 Android 智能体，它将推送给那五个买了 Pixel 手机的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: You don't work for Google.
zh: 你不为 Google 工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to go in a slightly different direction.
zh: 我想稍微换个方向。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something that I'm curious if you're following is just the anti-AI sentiment that feels like is growing.
zh: 我好奇你是否在关注的一件事是这种反 AI 情绪，感觉正在增长。

speaker: Unknown
en: It feels like if you've seen these surveys, AI is less popular than ICE, people are trying to stop data centers from being built.
zh: 感觉好像如果你看过这些调查，AI 比 ICE 更不受欢迎，人们正试图阻止建设数据中心。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think Eric Schmidt just did a commencement speech and people were booing him every time he mentioned AI.
zh: 我想 Eric Schmidt 刚做了一个毕业演讲，人们每次他提到 AI 都会对他喝倒彩。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just like, "What do you think is going on?
zh: 就像，“你觉得这是怎么回事？

speaker: Unknown
en: Where do you think this goes over time?"
zh: 你觉得随着时间的推移这会走向何方？”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's interesting.
zh: 很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's a big sort of fuzzy mass of different stuff, I think.
zh: 这是一个巨大的各种不同东西的模糊团块，我想。

speaker: Unknown
